---
title: Shiny Widgets
filters:
  - shinylive
---

[Shiny for Python](https://shiny.posit.co/py/) and the [shinylive](https://github.com/quarto-ext/shinylive) Quarto extension 
enable creating apps that run in the browser embedded directly into the Quarto document. 

## Examples

### Minimal example

Below is a minimal widget with just a slider that controls a small calculation.

```{shinylive-python}
#| standalone: true
from shiny import *

app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.input_slider("n", "N", 0, 100, 40),
    ui.output_text_verbatim("txt"),
)

def server(input, output, session):
    @output
    @render.text
    def txt():
        return f"The value of n*2 is {input.n() * 2}"

app = App(app_ui, server)
```

The code for this app is 

````python
```{shinylive-python}
#| standalone: true
from shiny import *

app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.input_slider("n", "N", 0, 100, 40),
    ui.output_text_verbatim("txt"),
)

def server(input, output, session):
    @output
    @render.text
    def txt():
        return f"The value of n*2 is {input.n() * 2}"

app = App(app_ui, server)
```
````

### Minimal example with editor

The widget below is equivalent to the one above, but here with an interactive editor enabled. This editor can be used to 
change the code of the widget interactively in the browser.

::: {.column-page-inset-right}

```{shinylive-python}
#| standalone: true
#| components: [editor, viewer]
from shiny import *

app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.input_slider("n", "N", 0, 100, 40),
    ui.output_text_verbatim("txt"),
)

def server(input, output, session):
    @output
    @render.text
    def txt():
        return f"The value of n*2 is {input.n() * 2}"

app = App(app_ui, server)
```
:::

### Less minimal example

This example is based on the example from the [Shinylive repository](https://github.com/quarto-ext/shinylive/blob/main/index.qmd). 
The widget lets you control the amplitude $A$, phase $\varphi$ and period $T$ of a sine wave

$$
s(A, T, \varphi) = A \cdot \sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{T} \cdot \frac{t - \varphi}{2}\right)
$$


::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{shinylive-python}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 420


from shiny import App, render, ui
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.layout_sidebar(
        ui.sidebar(
            ui.input_slider("period", "Period", 0.5, 2, 1, step=0.5),
            ui.input_slider("amplitude", "Amplitude", 0, 2, 1, step=0.25),
            ui.input_slider("shift", "Phase shift", 0, 2, 0, step=0.1),
        ),
        ui.output_plot("plot"),
    ),
)


def server(input, output, session):
    @output
    @render.plot(alt="Sine function")
    def plot():
        t = np.arange(0.0, 4.0, 0.01)
        s = input.amplitude() * np.sin(
            (2 * np.pi / input.period()) * (t - input.shift() / 2)
        )
        fig, ax = plt.subplots()
        ax.set_ylim([-2, 2])
        ax.plot(t, s)
        ax.grid()


app = App(app_ui, server)
```
:::
### Biological example: Genetic drift

This widget simulates how the frequency of one neutral trait variant can change from one generation to the next.
There is no selection or mutation in this model: each new generation is just a random sample from the previous one.
Smaller populations tend to drift more strongly because random sampling has a larger effect.

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{shinylive-python}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 520

from shiny import App, reactive, render, ui
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.colors import LinearSegmentedColormap, Normalize


app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.layout_sidebar(
        ui.sidebar(
            ui.input_slider(
                "population_size",
                "Population size",
                min=20,
                max=500,
                value=100,
                step=10,
            ),
            ui.input_slider(
                "starting_frequency",
                "Starting frequency",
                min=0.05,
                max=0.95,
                value=0.5,
                step=0.05,
            ),
            ui.input_slider(
                "generations",
                "Generations",
                min=10,
                max=200,
                value=50,
                step=10,
            ),
            ui.input_slider(
                "replicates",
                "Simulated populations",
                min=1,
                max=50,
                value=20,
                step=1,
            ),
            ui.input_action_button("resimulate", "Resimulate"),
        ),
        ui.output_plot("plot"),
        ui.output_text_verbatim("summary"),
    ),
)


def simulate_drift(population_size, starting_frequency, generations, replicates, seed):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    frequencies = np.zeros((replicates, generations + 1))
    frequencies[:, 0] = starting_frequency

    for generation in range(1, generations + 1):
        previous = frequencies[:, generation - 1]
        variant_counts = rng.binomial(population_size, previous)
        frequencies[:, generation] = variant_counts / population_size

    return frequencies


def server(input, output, session):
    @reactive.calc
    def trajectories():
        return simulate_drift(
            population_size=input.population_size(),
            starting_frequency=input.starting_frequency(),
            generations=input.generations(),
            replicates=input.replicates(),
            seed=input.resimulate(),
        )

    @output
    @render.plot(alt="Genetic drift trajectories")
    def plot():
        frequencies = trajectories()
        generation_axis = np.arange(input.generations() + 1)
        final_frequencies = frequencies[:, -1]
        frequency_colors = LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
            "frequency_colors",
            ["#d95f02", "#acacac", "#1b9e77"],
        )
        norm = Normalize(vmin=0, vmax=1)

        fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(1200, 800))
        for trajectory, final_frequency in zip(frequencies, final_frequencies):
            ax.plot(
                generation_axis,
                trajectory,
                color=frequency_colors(norm(final_frequency)),
                alpha=0.75,
            )
        ax.axhline(
            input.starting_frequency(),
            color="#4d4d4d",
            linestyle="--",
            linewidth=1,
            alpha=0.7,
        )
        ax.set(
            xlabel="Generation",
            ylabel="Variant frequency",
            ylim=(-0.02, 1.02),
            xlim=(0, input.generations()),
        )
        ax.grid(alpha=0.25)
        fig.colorbar(
            plt.cm.ScalarMappable(norm=norm, cmap=frequency_colors),
            ax=ax,
            label="Final variant frequency",
        )

    @output
    @render.text
    def summary():
        final_frequencies = trajectories()[:, -1]
        lost = np.sum(final_frequencies == 0)
        took_over = np.sum(final_frequencies == 1)
        total = input.replicates()

        return (
            f"By the final generation, the variant was lost in {lost} of \n"
            f"{total} simulated populations and took over in {took_over}."
        )


app = App(app_ui, server)
```
:::

## Technical Details

To enable the use of Shiny-apps the Shiny-live Quarto extension needs to be installed 

```default
quarto add quarto-ext/shinylive
```

And the project needs to have the `shinylive` Python package installed, 

```default
pixi add shinylive --pypi
```

Finally, either the document or the project needs to include the filter. For a specific document (`.qmd`-file),
this is done with 

```default
---
title: My ShinyLive enabled page
filters:
  - shinylive
---
```

Finally, it can be easier to design nice widgets with more screen space than the central 
column Quarto allocates by default, this can be controlled by wrapping the Shinylive block with a 
container, like

```default
::: {.column-page-inset-right}

... Code 

:::
```
See [Quarto: Article Layout](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/article-layout.html) for more examples of containers.
